Tafseer:
Alif-Lam-Ra. For a comprehensive discussion on the abbreviated letters (Huroof-e-Muqatta'at), please see the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah.

'A Book which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], that you might bring mankind out of darknesses into the light...'

The Meaning of Darkness and Light: 'Darknesses' (Zulumat) refers to the darkness of false beliefs and corrupt deeds, while 'Light' (Noor) refers to the light of faith and righteous deeds. There are thousands of paths of misguidance, but the path of guidance is only one; for this reason, 'darknesses' is plural and 'light' is singular.

Guidance Depends on Divine Enablement: '...by permission of their Lord.' That is, emerging from darknesses into the light will only be possible for anyone through the enablement (tawfeeq) granted by God. He, according to His sunnah, will grant guidance to those whom He finds worthy of it, and those whom He does not find worthy, He will leave to wander blindly in their misguidance. This means that the Prophet's responsibility in this matter is solely to convey and invite. Bringing people to the path of guidance is not his responsibility at all.

The Meaning of Al-Aziz and Al-Hameed: '...to the path of the Exalted in Might, the Praiseworthy.' This clarifies the light—showing that it refers to the path that leads to God, the Exalted in Might (Al-Aziz) and the Praiseworthy (Al-Hameed). Al-Aziz means the One Who is dominant and powerful over all, and for this reason, He alone is worthy of being feared. Al-Hameed means the source of all benevolence and grace, and for this reason, He alone is deserving of being praised and having hopes pinned on Him.

Commentary on Verses 2 and 3: 'Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And woe to the disbelievers from a severe punishment - The ones who prefer the worldly life over the Hereafter and avert [people] from the way of Allah, seeking to make it deviant. Those are in extreme error.'

Worldly Interests as a Veil to Guidance: That is, toward the path of this God Who alone is the owner of everything in the heavens and the earth. For this reason, those who are today denying this scripture of guidance, relying on their assumed partners, are inviting the destruction of a severe punishment upon themselves. 'The ones who prefer the worldly life over the Hereafter'—this uncovers the actual cause of their denial: they may make up whatever excuses they wish, but the real thing that has become a veil between them and this scripture of guidance is that they are not ready to sacrifice their worldly interests for the sake of the Hereafter. 'And avert [people] from the way of Allah'—consequently, they themselves are turning away from Allah's path and are also trying, to the extent of their power, to block others from it. 'And seeking to make it deviant'—that is, they are distorting the straight path leading to the Exalted in Might, the Praiseworthy God, turning it toward their assumed deities, and trying to convince people that if God can be reached, it is only through these winding, complex pathways. 'Those are in extreme error'—meaning that due to these actions of theirs, they have wandered very far away from the main highway.

'And We sent not a messenger except in the language of his people to make [the message] clear for them. Then Allah sends astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.'

This is an expression of the favor Allah has bestowed upon them by sending this scripture of guidance in Arabic and raising a Messenger from among their own nation. The purpose is to draw their attention to the fact that they should appreciate this blessing of Allah—that He sent down His scripture in their own language through a person from among themselves so that he might clearly explain Allah's guidance to the people.

The Divine Sunnah Regarding Guidance and Misguidance: 'Then Allah sends astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.' This clarifies Allah's sunnah regarding guidance and misguidance—that He alone guides whom He wills and leaves astray whom He wills. This is consolation for the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) showing that your duty is only to make God's words clearly understood, which you have done and are doing. As for people's believing or not believing, that depends on God's will, and God is the Exalted in Might, the Wise. That is, His every will is accompanied by His wisdom. Those who prove worthy of receiving guidance under His wisdom will receive guidance, and those who do not prove worthy of it will remain deprived. If a person utilizes the capabilities that Allah has placed within everyone, he becomes deserving of further enablement (tawfeeq); and if a person does not appreciate them, then—far from receiving more—even those capabilities that were given to him are snatched away. We have explained this divine sunnah in more than one place.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 5 to 8: In the following, reference is made to the story of Moses—that Allah had sent him too with the same purpose: to bring his people out of darknesses into the light. Consequently, he performed this task, and Allah's help and support saved them from the tyrannical grip of Pharaoh's people, while also informing them of God's decision: if you appreciate your Lord's blessing and remain grateful to Him, He will increase His favor upon you; and if you show ingratitude, you will face God's severe punishment. This reminder was intended to warn the Jews of the time who, in their passion of opposition to Islam, were throwing all their weight into the scale of the Quraysh. Just as the Quraysh had cast the teachings of Abraham behind their backs, the Jews were closing their ears to these warnings of Moses. Read the verses:

'And We certainly sent Moses with Our signs, [saying],

Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful.'

'With Our signs' refers to the signs of the staff, the shining hand, etc., which have been mentioned in great detail in various other places.

The Meaning of the Days of Allah: 'And remind them of the days of Allah.' When the word 'days' (Ayyam) is used in this manner, it refers to major, important historical and memorable days. For example, 'Ayyam-ul-Arab' (Days of the Arabs) refers to the battles of the Arabs. Similarly, 'Ayyam-ullah' (Days of Allah) refers to those memorable historical days in which Allah sent down His punishment upon disobedient nations and saved His believing servants from their tyranny and oppression. 'Indeed in that...'—meaning that in this story of Moses, there are great signs for those who remain steadfast and show gratitude. When Moses raised the call of truth, he and his people had to suffer great atrocities at the hands of Pharaoh's people, but ultimately Allah drowned the hosts of Pharaoh and saved the Children of Israel. The meaning is: similarly, Muslims are also facing a stage of trial at this time. If they pass through this stage with steadfastness, success will kiss their feet, and they will be grateful for the favors and blessings of their Lord.
'And [remember] when Moses said to his people,

'Aal' Means Followers and Adherents: We have clarified in another place that the word 'Aal' (family/people) is also used in the sense of followers and adherents. This refers to the speech that Moses delivered to his people when they had been saved from the clutches of Pharaoh's hosts. Moses's purpose in this speech was to remind his people that this was a very great trial from which God saved you purely out of His grace; so remember this grace, remain constantly grateful to God, and do not forget Him to lose yourselves in the same self-indulgences that brought you punishment at the hands of Pharaoh's people. This speech of Moses is recalled to the Jews of the Prophet's time so they may be warned that, having forgotten this instruction of Moses, they have walked again on the path of Satan and are inviting their own doom by opposing Islam.
'And [remember] when your Lord proclaimed,

Warning to the Jews of the Time: 'Ta'azzana' means to inform and make aware. The meaning is: Moses spoke what has passed above, and at the same time, your Lord also made it clear to you that if you remain grateful to Him by obeying His commands and instructions, He will constantly increase your numbers and His favors upon you; and if you walk on the path of ingratitude and disobedience, then remember that His punishment is also very severe. This is also brought here to warn the Jews of the Prophet's time, and it is clear from the address 'your Lord' (Rabbukum) that it is directed to them.
'And Moses said,

Increase in Favors Upon the Grateful: That is, at the same time, Moses also gave this warning: this demand for gratitude is not because God is in need of it, or because it adds anything to His glory and grandeur. He is independent of everything and is Praiseworthy in all circumstances. This gratitude is beneficial for the servants themselves because, as mentioned in the previous verse, through it Allah increases His favors and blessings upon them.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 9 to 17: Although the subsequent address is general, the direction of speech is toward the Muslims. They are assured that the stories of all the messengers and their nations testify that although trials are frequently encountered in the path of truth—to the extent that sometimes one must leave one's home and hearth—ultimate success belongs to the believers alone. Read the subsequent verses in this light:

'Has there not reached you the news of those before you - the people of Noah and 'Aad and Thamud and those after them? No one knows them but Allah. Their messengers came to them with clear proofs, but they returned their hands to their mouths and said,

Reference to Some Nations After Noah, 'Aad, and Thamud: 'And those after them? No one knows them but Allah'—this refers to those nations who came after Noah, 'Aad, and Thamud, to whom Allah sent His messengers, but the Qur'an has not mentioned them. No reliable account of them exists in historical records either. True knowledge of them belongs to Allah alone. In another place, this reality is stated thus: 'And We have already sent messengers before you. Among them are those [whose stories] We have related to you, and among them are those [whose stories] We have not related to you.'

Attempt to Shut the Messengers' Mouths: 'But they returned their hands to their mouths.' That is, they tried to shut the mouths of their messengers. When a person wants to stop someone from speaking, out of anger and hatred, he places his hand on the speaker's mouth, implying: 'Shut up, do not utter another single word.' 'Raddoo' (returned) is used here in the sense of 'Ja'aloo' (placed), and the word is used in this sense.

'Indeed, we disbelieve in that with which you have been sent, and indeed we are, about that to which you invite us, in disquieting doubt.' That is, we flatly deny the warning message with which you claim to have been sent by God. Neither do we accept you as God's messengers, nor are we ready to believe your threat that we will be destroyed if we do not accept your word. 'And indeed we are...'—and regarding this call to monotheism and belief in the Resurrection that you are presenting to us, we are in severe doubt about it.
'Their messengers said,
They said,

Nature of the Question of Astonishment: This question from the messengers is of an astonishing nature. The meaning is: Can there be any doubt about the God Who brought the heavens and the earth into existence? You already believe in God and accept Him as the Creator of the heavens and the earth, and we are calling you to Him alone, so there is no room for any doubt concerning Him. As for those whom you have associated as partners with God, we restrict you from them because there is no proof in their support.

An Invitation of Mercy: 'He invites you that He may forgive you of your sins and delay you for a specified term.' That is, this call which He is presenting to you through us is not something to be angry about; rather, it is a great mercy for you. He wants that if you accept this call, He will forgive your sins, grant you respite of life, and avert from you that punishment which you have invited upon yourselves by your misdeeds and which will surely fall upon you if you deny us.

Objection to Human Status: 'They said,

' That is, after hearing these statements of their messengers, if they spoke at all, they said: 'You are only humans like us, and through these speeches, you want to stop us from worshipping the deities whom our forefathers have been worshipping; so how can we accept such a major thing on the authority of humans like ourselves? If you want us to accept your word, show us some such miracle seeing which everyone would exclaim that indeed you are God's messengers and whatever you are saying is from Him.'
Commentary on Verses 11 and 12: 'Their messengers said to them,

The Messengers' Reply: This is the reply of the messengers: if you object to our human status, we confess that we are indeed humans like you. We do not claim to be superhuman. But Allah bestows His favor upon whichever of His servants He wills, and elevates them to the office of prophethood and messengership. This is the very favor that has been bestowed upon us; besides this, we make no other claim.

We have clarified in another place that God's every will is accompanied by His wisdom. For this reason, 'upon whom He wills' (ala man yashaa') does not mean that He arbitrarily makes anyone a prophet; rather, it means that from among His servants, He selects for this great task those whom He finds fit for this office according to His wisdom. 'And it has never been for us to bring you an authority except by permission of Allah.' That is, if—due to our human status—you are not ready to accept us as messengers without seeing a clear miracle at our hands, then this is not within our power. We can show a miracle only by God's command. 'And upon Allah let the believers rely.' So, we leave this demand of yours to Allah; if He wills, He will show a miracle, and if He does not, He will not. It is befitting for believers to rely on Allah in such matters.

The Basis of Trust (Tawakkul): 'And why should we not rely upon Allah while He has guided us to our ways...?' This is the argument for trusting in God: we have complete trust, and we ought to have trust, that He will help and guide us in overcoming whatever obstacles and hindrances we face in the paths upon which He Himself has guided us to walk. Your demand is also among these obstacles and hindrances. We trust that Allah will help us in this matter too. Our responsibility is to show steadfastness in this path, so we are determined that we will surely be patient against whatever harm you cause us, and we will rely on Allah, because Allah alone is the Being on Whom those who trust must rely.
Commentary on Verses 13 and 14: 'And those who disbelieved said to their messengers,
So their Lord revealed to them,

The Threat of the Nations to Their Messengers: Ultimately, in the life of every messenger, this stage has occurred: his nation, fed up with his call, gave him notice: 'Either return to our religion, or we will exile you from our land.' When matters reached this point, Allah comforted His messengers through revelation: 'We will surely destroy the wrongdoers and make you dwell in the land after them.' 'That is for he who fears My position and fears My threat'—meaning that this great glad tiding is for those who remained so fearful of standing before Me and of My warning that, in comparison, they cared nothing for any calamity or threat. 'Or you must return to our religion' does not imply that any messenger was ever a follower of the religion of ignorance at any point in his life. Prophets remain established on the primordial nature (Fitrat-ullah) even before their advent, but since there was no reason for the nation to show hostility toward them before they began their call, the people of the nation assumed them to be on their own religion. Later, when they began the call, the wicked of the nation began to criticize them for deviating from the religion.

Warning for the Followers of the Messengers: 'That is for he who fears My position and fears My threat' contains a warning and an exhortation to steadfastness for the followers of the messengers. Since the glad tiding given to the messengers in this verse also includes their followers, they are informed that this glad tiding is for those who face all kinds of trials and do not ignore the fear of God out of fear of their opponents.

'And they sought victory, and disappointed was every obstinate tyrant.'

Prayer for the Decision Between Truth and Falsehood from the Messenger: In the heat of denial, the disbelievers repeatedly demanded from their messengers: 'Bring that punishment with which you are repeatedly threatening us, so that this matter may be decided.' But the messengers of Allah, due to their compassion, kept praying for them to be blessed with faith. However, everything has a limit. Ultimately, a stage came when the messengers also prayed: 'Our Lord, judge between us and our people in truth, and You are the best of judges.'

Commentary on Verses 16 and 17: The Subsequent Punishment for the Rebellious: That is, for such rebellious ones and tyrants, this punishment of the world is not all; rather, Hell also stands ready before them, where they will be given purulent water (pus and blood) to drink. Yet, such will be their thirst that they will sip it and try to swallow it, but will not be able to swallow it. Death will be coming upon them from every side, but death will not reach them to give them escape from this punishment; and this is not all—further severe punishment will be ahead of them.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 18 to 23: The Subsequent States: In the following, details are given of the conditions that these disbelievers and polytheists will face in the Hereafter. Followers will curse their leaders, and leaders will curse their followers—to the extent that Satan too will clearly tell his followers: 'Do not blame me; rather, whoever followed me should today beat his own head over his misfortune.' In contrast, the state of the believers is described: they will be seated in Paradise, exchanging greetings of peace and safety. Read the verses:

'The example of those who disbelieved in their Lord is [that] their deeds are like ashes which the wind blows forcefully on a stormy day; they are unable [to keep] from what they earned a thing. That is the extreme error.'

Polytheism in Reality is Disbelief: 'Those who disbelieved' refers to the polytheists who are addressed here. We have clarified in another place that polytheism (shirk) in its reality is disbelief (kufr). In religion, only that belief in God is valid which is accompanied by monotheism (tawheed). If any other is associated as a partner in His essence, His attributes, or His rights, then such belief is invalid. It is equivalent to denying Him.

An Illustration of the Polytheists' Deeds: This is an illustration of the deeds of these polytheists—showing that this will be the state of their deeds on the Day of Resurrection. Clearly, 'deeds' here refers to those actions which they performed assuming them to be good deeds. It is said that on the Day of Resurrection, their deeds will be like a heap of ashes upon which a fierce wind blows on a stormy day and scatters it entirely. 'They are unable [to keep] from what they earned a thing'—meaning that from this earning of theirs which they did alongside polytheism, nothing is going to remain in their hands; all of it will turn to dust and fly away, and only its burden will remain in their share. 'That is the extreme error'—that is, one lostness, deviation, and deprivation is that after which there remains a possibility of returning and finding the path, but this is a far-reaching lostness and deprivation after which there remains absolutely no possibility of returning and finding the way. Cast a glance back at verse 3 of this Surah; and a very sublime illustration of the deeds of such people is also present in verse 39 of Surah Al-Noor.

Commentary on Verses 19 and 20: 'Have you not seen that Allah created the heavens and the earth in truth? If He wills, He can do away with you and bring in a new creation. And that is not difficult for Allah.'

Threat to the Quraysh: This is an explicit threat to the Quraysh: have you not considered that this world is not a plaything of an amusement-seeker, but Allah created it with a purpose and end? Your existence has become completely contrary to this purpose and end, so why would He keep carrying you on the back of this earth when His power is such that if He wills, He can destroy all of you in the blink of an eye and raise a new creation? 'And that is not difficult for Allah'—that is, do not assume that this task is difficult for God or that it will be heavy upon Him. There is no limit to His power, and alongside benevolence and mercy, He is also the Establisher of justice and equity; so why would it be heavy upon Him to destroy those who have completely eradicated justice and equity in every sphere of life?
'And they will come out [for judgement] before Allah all together, and the weak will say to those who were arrogant,
They will say,

Presence in the Hereafter: 'And they will come out [for judgement] before Allah all together.' The word 'baraza' basically means to emerge from behind a veil. The eloquence in using this word here is that a time will come when all those who in this world make their assumed partners and intercessors a veil and shield, and those who act as protectors and shields, will all stand before the One God, and there no one will be anyone's protector, companion, or helper. At that time, the weak will say to their leaders: 'In the world, we were your followers; we obeyed whatever you commanded, and as a result, this punishment stands before us.' 'So can you avail us anything against the punishment of Allah?'—So will you lighten some of our burden from this punishment? 'Agna anhu' means 'kafaahu' (sufficed him).

The Leaders' Reply: 'They will say,

' The major ones and leaders will reply: 'Whatever we ourselves were, we made you the same. If we ourselves had been on guidance, we would have shown you its path. Now, the time for complaints has passed; this is the time to face the results, and these are so definitive that whether we cry out or show patience, neither will they be averted, nor is there any way of escape left for us.'
'And Satan will say when the matter has been concluded,
Satan's Reply: 'And Satan will say when the matter has been concluded,

' That is, when the case of the leaders of disbelief and their followers is finished, and Satan sees how they are arguing among themselves, he will surely fear that both these parties, having finished their mutual dispute, will turn upon him to curse and blame him. Consequently, he will take the lead to silence them first. The first thing he will say is: 'The promise of reward and punishment that God made to you was a true promise; He did not violate His promise but fulfilled it. On the other hand, the green pastures I showed you were merely lies and deception, and I did not fulfill any of those promises.' His second point will be: 'But I had no authority over you except that I invited you...'—'After all, I had no power to put you on the path of misguidance; if I had any power, it was only that I could invite and tempt you to misguidance. If you chose this misguidance, you did so of your own choice and will; so do not blame me in this matter, but blame yourselves.' The third thing he will say is: 'I cannot be called to your aid, nor can you be called to my aid...'—'Now, nothing is achieved by blaming each other; today, neither can I help you, nor can you help me. So before you start blaming me, I declare my disassociation from your making me a partner to God and associating me in His obedience before.'
'And those who believed and did righteous deeds will be admitted to gardens beneath which rivers flow, abiding eternally therein by permission of their Lord. Their greeting therein will be,

'

The End of the Believers: After clarifying the end of the people of disbelief and polytheism, this describes the end of the believers—showing that they will be in Paradise, welcoming one another with words of welcome, appreciation, peace, and mercy, because they gave correct advice to one another, the result of which stands before them in the best form. Read this verse in contrast to the mutual cursing of the disbelievers mentioned above; only then will its true impact emerge.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 24 to 27: Illustration of Monotheism and Polytheism: In the verses above, the illustration of polytheistic and disbelieving deeds was described. Now, in the following verses, the illustration of polytheistic beliefs and concepts versus monotheism and its beliefs is described—so as to clarify that polytheism has no foundation within intellect and human nature, nor does it have any root with God, and for this reason, all deeds based on it will vanish into the air. In contrast, the foundation of monotheism (tawheed) exists in intellect and human nature, as well as with Allah; its roots are deep in the earth, and its branches are spread in the air. For this reason, Allah will grant steadfastness and establishing power in both this world and the Hereafter to those who stand by monotheism. Read the verses:

Commentary on Verses 24 and 25: 'Have you not considered how Allah presents an example, [making] a good word like a good tree, whose root is firmly fixed and its branches [high] in the sky? It produces its fruit all the time by permission of its Lord...'

The Address 'Have you not considered' (Alam tara): Although the word is singular, the address is general. It is as if every individual of the addressed group is being addressed individually to ponder over the illustrations that follow.

The Meaning of 'Good Word' (Kalimah Tayyibah): 'Good Word' refers to the word of monotheism (Tawheed) and the beliefs and concepts based on it.

The Meaning of 'Good Tree' (Shajarah Tayyibah): 'Good Tree' refers to a tree that is fruitful, shady, beneficial, and blessed.

Omission of Contrasting Words: In 'whose root is firmly fixed and its branches [high] in the sky,' the contrasting words for the mentioned terms are omitted. For example, in the first phrase, the word 'fixed' (thabit) is made explicit, so in the second phrase, its contrasting word 'high' (aal) or a similar word is omitted after 'its branches'. Similarly, in the second phrase, 'in the sky' (fis-samaa') is made explicit, so in the first phrase, 'in the earth' (fil-ard) is omitted after 'fixed'. Examples of such omissions are abundant in the Qur'an and Arabic literature. Its benefit is that fewer words are used, and deeper meanings are encompassed within them. If we unfold these omissions and translate, the translation would be: 'Its root is deep within the depths of the earth, and its branches are spread out in the space of the sky.' The word 'samaa'' is used for the space of the air just as it is used for the sky. Since a tree receives nourishment and strength from both the earth and the air, it is said: 'whose root is firmly fixed and its branches [high] in the sky'—meaning that because its roots are deep in the earth, it receives full nourishment from the earth, and because its branches are spread out in the air, the air also plays its full part in its nourishment.

The Word of Monotheism is Evergreen: 'It produces its fruit all the time by permission of its Lord.' This describes the evergreen nature of this tree—showing that autumn never comes to this tree; rather, it always yields fruit by the command of its Lord.

The Illustration of the Word of Monotheism: By comparing the word of monotheism to such a tree, the Qur'an has clarified one reality: its roots are deeply set within human intellect and nature, and with Allah too, this is the reality holding the greatest value. It is as if the status it possesses in both earth and heaven is not possessed by anything else. The second reality clarified is that it constantly receives nourishment and strength from within human nature, and showers of grace also constantly descend upon it from above, keeping it always green and fresh. The third reality clarified is that its blessings are eternal and permanent; its benefit is always ongoing. The one in whose chest this light exists remains always satisfied and joyful.

Warning to the Addressed and Reminder of Favor: 'And Allah presents examples for the people that perhaps they will be reminded.' This is both a warning to the addressees (the Quraysh) and a reminder of favor—showing that We are presenting these parables to them so they may take heed and consider what high status of honor Allah wants to grant them, yet they want to remain in the dirt into which Satan has pushed them. Keep in view that since realities assume a metaphorical garb in parables, they are of great importance in bringing meanings closer to understanding. This is why prophets and callers to truth have made great use of them, though unfortunately, the ignorant have never appreciated them.

'And the example of a bad word is like a bad tree, uprooted from the surface of the earth, not having any stability.'

The Meaning of 'Bad Word' (Kalimah Khabeethah): 'Bad Word' refers to the word of polytheism (shirk) and the beliefs and concepts based on polytheism and disbelief.

The Meaning of 'Bad Tree' (Shajarah Khabeethah): 'Bad Tree' refers to a wild, useless thorny bush which has neither flowers, fruit, shade, nor food. If you touch it, its thorns injure your hands; if you taste it, its bitterness numbs the tongue; if you sit near it, its odor offends the sense of smell. 'Uprooted from the surface of the earth, not having any stability'—meaning that it has no root in the earth. If you want to uproot it, you can uproot it right from the surface of the earth; it has no stability at all, and for that which has no stability in the earth, there is no possibility of spreading in the air.

The Illustration of the Word of Polytheism: Then, the illustration of the word of polytheism is described—showing that its example is like self-growing, thorny, foul-smelling, useless, and fruitless bushes. Neither do they have any deep root, nor do they have any spread in the air. If someone wants to uproot them, he can uproot them right from the top. From this illustration, it becomes clear that polytheism has no foundation in intellect, human nature, or in the religion revealed by God. Like self-growing bad bushes, it crops up wherever it finds space. If there are hands ready to uproot it, they can easily uproot and throw it away, but if there are no hands to uproot it, it occupies a lot of space.

Glad Tiding for the Believers: This also contains a glad tiding for the believers—showing that the life of the bushes of polytheism and disbelief spread around them is no longer long. God has raised those hands which will very soon cleanse this land of these unstable and bad bushes.

'Allah keeps firm those who believe, with the firm word, in worldly life and in the Hereafter. And Allah sends astray the wrongdoers. And Allah does what He wills.'

'Firm Word' (Qawl-e-Thabit) Refers to the Word of Monotheism: 'Firm word' refers to the same word of monotheism whose stability and firmness in heaven, earth, nature, and the universe have been praised above. In 'And Allah sends astray the wrongdoers,' sending astray (idlal) means making efforts and hard work go in vain, just as in verse 1 of Surah Muhammad: 'Those who disbelieved and averted [people] from the way of Allah - He wastefully lost their deeds.' 'Wrongdoers' (zalimeen) refers to the polytheists because polytheism (shirk), as explicitly stated in the Qur'an, is the greatest wrong.

The meaning is: by virtue of the firm and established word of monotheism, Allah will grant stability and firmness to the believers in both this world and the Hereafter. As for the polytheists, Allah will destroy all their deeds like ashes and dust, as mentioned above. 'And Allah does what He wills'—meaning that God will do whatever He wills; no one will be able to hold His hand, nor will any effort or intercession avail anything with Him. Keep in view what we have mentioned repeatedly: God's will is always accompanied by His justice and His wisdom.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 28 to 34: Warning to the Quraysh and Glad Tiding to the Believers: In the following, in very clear words, there is first a threat and warning to the Quraysh—that since they have thrown their nation into the pit of destruction, their abode is Hell. After this, there is a glad tiding of success for the Muslims, and at the same time, an indication to the preparation they must make for this success. Read the subsequent verses in this light:

Commentary on Verses 28 to 30: 'Have you not opposed those who exchanged the favor of Allah for disbelief and settled their people [in] the home of ruin? [It is] Hell, which they will [enter to] burn, and wretched is the settlement. And they attributed to Allah equals to mislead [people] from His way. Say,

A Specific Context for the Use of 'Have you not considered' (Alam tara): In the address 'Alam tara', there is also to some extent an aspect of expressing glory and astonishment. Here, the context is proof that the occasion is one of expressing astonishment and blame, pointing to the Quraysh and their leaders, because in Arabia, they were the ones who held the position of throwing their nation—as clear from 'and settled their people [in] the home of ruin'—into the pit of polytheism and disbelief, thereby preparing Hell for themselves and for others.

The Quraysh's Ingratitude for Favors: 'Who exchanged the favor of Allah for disbelief'—that is, they received all blessings purely by Allah's grace and mercy, and through the blessing of Abraham's prayer, as detailed below. God granted them leadership and command over all of Arabia by virtue of their guardianship of the Sanctuary (Haram); He granted them the peace of urban life in place of a nomadic, unstable life; He opened the doors of provision and favor for them in an uncultivated valley. Yet, the appreciation they showed for all this was that they adopted a life of disbelief and polytheism, creating many partners and intercessors, and turning the creation away from God toward them. 'Say,

'—Tell them to enjoy themselves for a few days, because ultimately their abode is Hell. Enjoying the blessings of God's table of bounty while bowing heads of devotion and gratitude before others is not something that will last long. This is a brief respite that will end very soon.

'Tell My servants who have believed to establish prayer and spend from what We have provided them, secretly and publicly, before a Day comes in which there is no exchange, nor any friendships.'

'Khilal' means friendship and mutual affection, and the word 'bay' (exchange) is used in some contexts to mean both buying and selling. When things are exchanged for things, both parties to the transaction are sellers (ba'i') and buyers (mushtari).

Message to the Muslims: After warning the Quraysh, this message is now for the Muslims: tell them to maintain prayer and spend in God's way both secretly and publicly. Secretly, because the actual spirit of spending in Allah's way is sincerity (ikhlas), and this is found in that spending which is done secretly; and publicly, because an aspect of this also brings blessing, which is that it encourages and inspires others to spend. Placing 'secretly' first shows that this type of spending is primary and better.

The Real Need for Spending: 'In which there is no exchange, nor any friendships' expresses the actual need for this spending: it is basically not demanded for the benefit of others, but is demanded for the salvation and success of the spender himself, because a time is coming in which neither buying and selling will occur, nor will friendship and relationship avail. If anything avails, it will be this spending which one performed in the way of Allah.

Glad Tiding of Future Success for the Muslims: If you ponder well over the words, style, and context of the verse, a glad tiding of future success for the Muslims is also implicit in it. Hidden in it is the point that the Quraysh destroyed all the purposes of the trust they inherited from Abraham (peace be upon him). Now, the opportunity is for the believers to prepare themselves for these purposes and become the trustees of this trust.

Commentary on Verses 32 to 34: 'It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth and sent down rain from the sky and produced thereby some fruits as provision for you and subjected for you the ships to sail upon the sea by His command and subjected for you the rivers. And He subjected for you the sun and the moon, both constant, and subjected for you the night and the day. And He gave you from all you asked of Him. And if you should count the favor of Allah, you could not enumerate them. Indeed, mankind is [generally] most unjust and ungrateful.'

This is a further explanation of the subject mentioned above—showing that all blessings were bestowed by Allah, yet the unjust polytheists associated each of them with their assumed deities and began to worship them. Rain, ships, rivers, sun, moon, night, and day—Allah has actively engaged all of them in benefiting man, but the ignorant made some of these things deities themselves, and those which they did not make deities, they placed under the control of other deities, calling out to them. We have clarified the meaning of 'subjected for you' (sakhkhara lakum) in another place: it means to engage a thing for someone's benefit. Heaven, earth, sun, and moon are all subjected in God's hand; however, God has engaged them for the benefit of man, and this is His grace and favor. 'And He subjected for you the sun and the moon, both constant.' 'Daa'ib' means constant. That is, they are busy performing their assigned duty in the same manner, on the same path, and in the same orbit, adhering strictly to seconds and minutes; it is impossible for even a hair's breadth of difference to occur. Consider: is this a sign of their being deities, or of their being subjected and subdued by the actual Ruler of the universe, the Exalted in Might, the All-Knowing God? 'And He gave you from all you asked of Him'—this address is to man as a human being. The meaning is: according to your nature and creation, whatever things you were made in need of, Allah bestowed upon you according to His wisdom and interest. This perfect compatibility between demand and its response is itself the greatest testimony that He Who created you is the One Who arranged for all your needs. 'Indeed, mankind is [generally] most unjust and ungrateful.' Although the word 'man' (insan) is general, the direction of speech is toward the polytheists mentioned in the previous verse. Since they are not worthy of attention due to their unworthiness, the statement is put in a general form. 'Zaloom' means one who violates rights. We have clarified in another place that by committing polytheism, man violates the right of God as well as the right of his own self. 'Kaffar' means an ungrateful person—one who receives blessings from someone else but sings the praises of another.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 35 to 41: Clarification of the Religion of Abraham: The greatest argument that the Quraysh presented in support of their religion of polytheism was that this religion was inherited by them from their great forefather, Abraham. In the following, this claim is refuted, and the reality is clarified: Abraham migrated from a fertile, green area and settled his offspring in this uncultivated valley so they could establish a center for the worship of the One God, so that his offspring could remain free from all pollutions of polytheism and worship the One God, Who has no partner. All blessings of peace and provision in this uncultivated valley are the result of Abraham's prayers, but the Quraysh have associated all these blessings with their deities, leaving the One God to worship their idols, calling this the religion of Abraham. Read the verses:

Commentary on Verses 35 and 36: 'And [remember] when Abraham said,

Abraham's Prayer: This is the prayer Abraham made at the time he settled Ishmael and his mother in the land of Makkah. First of all, he prayed for peace for this land because up to that time, this entire area was deprived of peace. Allah accepted this prayer of Abraham in such a way that—let alone humans—even harming any living creature in this land was made a sin. Then, by virtue of the Kaaba, four months were made sacred for Hajj and Umrah, during which caravans traveled from one part of the country to another without fear; no one could touch a hair on anyone's head. Furthermore, by virtue of their guardianship of the Kaaba, the Quraysh obtained the status of leadership and command in the whole country; their caravans traveled to every part of the country, and far from interfering with them, the tribes provided them with escorts.

The second prayer he made was: keep me and my offspring safe from the impurity of idol worship. This was the actual purpose for which Abraham had migrated. In this prayer, he included himself too, which shows the contagiousness and danger of polytheism—that even a great prophet like Abraham prayed to be kept safe from its touch. This reality is also expressed through 'My Lord, indeed they have led astray many among mankind'—showing that the fitnah of these idols is such that a vast multitude of people fell victim to it. This is a style of eloquence: when it is intended to express the intensity of influence and capture of something, they sometimes use the pronouns and verbs that are normally used for intelligent beings. In the prayer, the word 'my sons' (baniyya) is used, which literally means 'my sons', but this word is used primarily to mean 'my offspring'. The purpose is: keep my offspring safe. Consequently, later in this very prayer, the word 'offspring' (zurriyyah) is used, which is general. 'So whoever follows me - then he is of me; and whoever disobeys me - indeed, You are Forgiving and Merciful.' This is a declaration of disassociation from those of his offspring who deviate from his path and fall into polytheism and idol worship. He said: those who follow in this matter are indeed from me and in my group, and those who deviate from my path and fall into polytheism, their case is left to You; You will deal with them as You find them deserving; You are Forgiving and Merciful, and no injustice is feared from You. Those who deserve mercy will not be deprived of it.

Keep in view that this is not a prayer of forgiveness from Abraham for those who commit polytheism, but merely leaving their case, as we have stated, to the Forgiving, Merciful God.

'Our Lord, I have settled some of my descendants in an uncultivated valley near Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may establish prayer. So make hearts among the people incline toward them and provide for them from the fruits that they might be grateful.'

The Real Purpose of Migration: That is, I have settled my offspring in a barren, uncultivated land near Your sacred House so they may maintain prayer. So, turn the hearts of people toward them and grant them fruits for sustenance so they may remain grateful to You. From this, one thing learned is that God's monotheism and His exclusive worship is something for the sake of which man can leave everything, and he must leave everything—to the extent that if he has to lead a solitary life in a barren land, he must choose it, but under no circumstances should he abandon God and His worship.

Establishing Prayer is the Purpose of Building the Kaaba: The second thing learned is that the Kaaba is originally the center of prayer; for this reason, the actual ones deserving of its guardianship are those who maintain prayer, not those who have destroyed monotheism and prayer yet claim its guardianship. Here, Abraham mentioned prayer as the primary purpose of building the Kaaba. Later, when he was commanded to call people to Hajj, it became the center of Hajj too, and of sacrifice in memory of Ishmael's sacrifice. Keep in view that the concept of 'establishing prayer' (iqamat-us-salat) does not merely mean performing prayer, but it also includes calling people to it and arranging and managing that people perform prayer.

The Actual Components of Abraham's Prayer: Here, due to the specific nature of prayer and this land, Abraham prayed for two things for his offspring. One was that You turn the hearts of people toward them. The effect of the acceptance of this prayer of Abraham was that the Kaaba very soon became the destination of all of Arabia, and after the advent of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it became the center of the entire world. The second prayer, due to the barren nature of the land, was for the expansion of provision and favor—the effect of which appeared very soon in the form of Makkah becoming the center of trade for all of Arabia. We have discussed 'And provide for them from the fruits' in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah.

'Our Lord, indeed You know what we conceal and what we declare, and nothing is hidden from Allah on the earth or in the heaven.'

'Our Lord, indeed You know what we conceal and what we declare'—the eloquence of this phrase cannot be fully described. When a servant prays to his Lord in an important matter, a major difficulty he faces is that there are some things he wants to say but cannot express; similarly, there are some things that are in the heart, but he feels some hesitation in saying them due to some vague reason. This phrase has encompassed all such things because God's knowledge encompasses both the manifest and the hidden.

'Praise to Allah, who has granted to me in old age Ishmael and Isaac. Indeed, my Lord is the Hearer of supplication.' By referring to the past great favors of Allah upon his prayer, he has, so to speak, brought a recommendation. The meaning is: the Lord Who granted me Ishmael and Isaac in old age, I hope from Him that just as He accepted my prayer regarding them, He will also accept this prayer of mine. I have never been deprived after asking Him.

Commentary on Verses 40 and 41: 'My Lord, make me an establisher of prayer, and [many] from my descendants. Our Lord, and accept my supplication. Our Lord, forgive me and my parents and the believers the Day the account is established.'

At the end, he prayed for himself and his offspring to remain active in this purpose for which they had built the Kaaba, and which was mentioned above in the words: 'Our Lord, that they may establish prayer.' In 'and [many] from my descendants' (wa min zurriyyahi), the 'min' is partitive because he had settled only Ishmael from among his offspring in Makkah. At the very end, he prayed for forgiveness for himself, his parents, and all believers. Regarding Abraham's father, Azar, it is explicitly stated in several places in the Qur'an that since Abraham had told him at the time of migration: 'I will pray for your forgiveness,' he kept praying for him despite his severe insistence on polytheism and disbelief. Later, when Allah restricted him from this, he stopped. It appears that this prayer mentioned above is from before this restriction was revealed.

Characteristics of the Prayer: In this prayer, see that the word 'Rabb' (Lord) or 'Rabbana' (Our Lord) has occurred seven times. Although this might seem like a repetition, it is actually among the characteristics, or rather the requirements, of prayer. The actual nature of prayer is humility, helplessness, seeking help, appeal, and crying out. This requires that the One to Whom the prayer is made be addressed repeatedly. When a servant addresses God as 'Rabbi' (My Lord), he makes that specific kindness a recommendation for his prayer, which he himself has experienced; and when he addresses Him as 'Rabbana' (Our Lord), he makes His general benevolence a recommendation for his prayer, which is witnessed in all creation.

Recall the point mentioned above: this prayer was narrated to the Quraysh so they might consider for what purpose they were settled in this barren valley and what they have become today. Moreover, they should also reflect on the fact that all those blessings they received in this land were obtained through the blessing of Abraham's prayer and purely by Allah's grace, yet they have attributed the source of all these blessings to their imaginary deities.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 42 to 52: Conclusion of the Surah: The following are the concluding verses of the Surah, in which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is consoled and there is a severe threat for the disbelievers. The connection of speech is very clear. Read the verses:

Commentary on Verses 42 and 43: 'And never think that Allah is unaware of what the wrongdoers do. He only delays them for a Day when eyes will stare [in horror]. Racing ahead, their heads uplifted, their glance not returning to them, and their hearts void.'

'Eyes will stare' (Tashkhasu fihil-absar)—'shakhasa' means elevation, but when it is used for eyes, it means that the eyes remain fixed or wide open in horror. 'Muhti'een'—'ih_ta' means to move quickly or rush toward something, particularly when this moving is due to fear and terror. 'Muqni'ee'—'iqna' means to raise the head or raise the voice. Here, it is used in the sense of walking with heads raised.

Address to the Prophet, Warning to the Quraysh: Although the address in the verse is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in terms of outward words, the severe warning in it is entirely directed toward the Quraysh. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is assured: do not assume that We are unaware of what your opponents are doing. We are not unaware. We are watching everything, but We are delaying their matter to that Day the terror of which will be such that when their eyes rise, they will remain fixed; they will not even be able to blink. With heads raised, they will be running quickly toward the place of Gathering, and their hearts will be flying away due to fear and terror.
'And, [O Muhammad], warn the people of a Day when the punishment will come to them and those who did wrong will say,
[But it will be said],

'

That is, if they do not listen to your word today, let them not listen; warn them of that Day when punishment will fall upon them, and these people who wronged themselves will cry out at that time: 'Our Lord, grant us a little more respite; we will accept Your call and follow Your messengers.' But at that time, the time for repentance and reform will have passed. They will be answered: 'Why, what happened now? Are you not the very ones who used to swear oaths that you would never shift from your stubbornness?'

'And you dwelt in the dwellings of those who wronged themselves, and it had become clear to you how We dealt with them. And We presented for you examples.'

Reminder of History: That is, it is not the case that you were unaware of what fate befell those who denied the messengers and how God dealt with them; you lived in the very dwellings of those destroyed and punished nations, and God narrated to you their lessons and warnings through His messengers. These events were not narrated to you merely as stories, but the purpose of narrating them was to show you that the same fate will be yours if you deny the Messenger. When all this has happened and you did not believe, for what are you asking further respite now?

'And they had planned their plan, but with Allah is their plan, even if their plan had been sufficient to do away with the mountains.' That is, those nations too, as far as planning is concerned, left no stone unturned; but today, all their plans are lying with God. Although these plans of theirs were so powerful that even mountains could be moved by them, God rendered all their plans useless.

'So never think that Allah will fail in His promise to His messengers. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Owner of Retribution.' This address is also to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and in this too, the aspect of solace and peace of mind is for the Prophet, while the aspect of warning and wrath is directed toward the disbelievers and polytheists. The meaning is: never assume that God will violate His promise of victory and support that He made to His messengers under any circumstances. He is the Exalted in Might, the Dominant—no one can hold His hand; and He is the Owner of Retribution, and for this reason, He will surely bring the wicked and the enemies of truth and justice to their fate.

'[Remember] the Day when the earth will be replaced with another earth, and the heavens [as well], and all creatures will come out before Allah, the One, the Prevailing.'

Reminder of the Day of Hereafter: 'The Day when the earth will be replaced with another earth, and the heavens [as well]'—meaning, the heavens will be replaced with other heavens. The meaning is: remind them of that Day when both this earth and this heaven will be replaced with another earth and other heavens, and all these people will stand before the One, Prevailing God. We have clarified the meaning of the word 'barazoo' in another place—showing that it also implies that on that Day, no one will have any helper, supporter, or intercessor with him. There will be only each individual and his deeds. The heavens and earth will also be different; there will be no fortresses, strongholds, castles, or palaces of anyone.

The Meaning of Al-Qahhar: We have clarified the meaning of the word 'Al-Qahhar' (the Prevailing/Subduer) in another place—showing that its actual meaning is: the One Who alone holds control over everyone, completely independent of anyone's help and support, whom no one has the power to escape.

Commentary on Verses 49 to 51: 'And you will see the criminals that Day bound together in shackles, their garments of liquid pitch and their faces covered by the Fire. So that Allah may recompense every soul for what it earned. Indeed, Allah is swift in account.'

'Sarabeel' is the plural of 'sirbal', which is used to mean a shirt, and it also comes in the general meaning of a garment. Here, the context is proof that it is used in the second, general meaning. The scholars of language have written various meanings for 'qatiran' (liquid pitch), but this word is well known for tar/tarcoal. In our view, it is used in this sense here. The blackening of the bodies and faces of those deserving of Hell is also mentioned in other places in the Qur'an. Moreover, how fire flares up on tarcoal is a well-known reality.

Further Detail of the Terror of the Day of Hereafter: This is a further detail of the terror of that Day—showing that on that Day, all criminals will be bound together in chains. Their garments will be of pitch, and the flames of fire will cover their faces. All this will happen so that every soul may receive the recompense of its deeds, and no one should think that taking the account of all creation will require a long time. God will settle everyone's account in the blink of an eye.

Final Warning: 'This [Quran] is a notification for the people that they may be warned thereby and that they may know that He is but one God and that those of understanding may be reminded.' This is the final warning—showing that all these matters have been conveyed to people so that God's argument may be fully established against them, people may be warned of the punishment and the Resurrection, and they may know well that they have to deal with only one God, that no one else is going to avail them, and that those who possess intellect may take heed.

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. And our last call is that all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
